examples.title: Basic Agile Iteration Loop
examples.description: Pseudocode representing the iterative nature of agile development
examples.code:
ALGORITHM AgileIteration
BEGIN
  SET sprintDuration = 2 TO 4 WEEKS
  WHILE productBacklog is not empty DO
    BEGIN
      // Sprint Planning
      SELECT top priority items FROM productBacklog
      CREATE sprintBacklog FROM selected items
      
      // Development Iteration
      FOR each day IN sprintDuration DO
        BEGIN
          CONDUCT daily standup meeting
          DEVELOP user stories in sprint backlog
          RUN unit tests on new code
          IF blocker encountered THEN
            RESOLVE blocker in daily standup
          END IF
        END FOR
      
      // Sprint Review
      DEMONSTRATE working increment to stakeholders
      GATHER feedback from stakeholders
      UPDATE product backlog with feedback
      
      // Sprint Retrospective
      REFLECT on sprint performance
      IDENTIFY improvements for next sprint
      ADJUST process accordingly
    END
  END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the agile iteration cycle: Sprint Planning selects priority items, Daily Standups occur each day, Development happens iteratively, Sprint Review gathers feedback, and Sprint Retrospective enables continuous improvement.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = number of iterations

examples.title: Agile User Story Point Estimation
examples.description: Python code demonstrating planning poker-style estimation for user stories
examples.code:
class UserStory:
    def __init__(self, title, description, priority):
        self.title = title
        self.description = description
        self.priority = priority  # 1 = highest, 5 = lowest
        self.points = None
        self.status = "Backlog"

class AgileSprint:
    def __init__(self, name, capacity):
        self.name = name
        self.capacity = capacity  # total story points
        self.stories = []
        self.velocity = 0
    
    def plan_sprint(self, product_backlog):
        """Select highest priority stories that fit within sprint capacity"""
        self.stories = []
        remaining_capacity = self.capacity
        
        # Sort by priority (highest priority first)
        sorted_backlog = sorted(product_backlog, key=lambda x: x.priority)
        
        for story in sorted_backlog:
            if story.points <= remaining_capacity and story.points is not None:
                self.stories.append(story)
                remaining_capacity -= story.points
                story.status = "In Sprint"
                
        self.velocity = sum(s.points for s in self.stories if s.points)
        return self.stories

# Example usage
backlog = [
    UserStory("Login Feature", "User can login with email", 1),
    UserStory("Search", "Search products by name", 2),
    UserStory("Cart", "Add items to shopping cart", 1),
    UserStory("Payment", "Process credit card payment", 1)
]

# Assign story points (Fibonacci: 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13)
backlog[0].points = 3
backlog[1].points = 5
backlog[2].points = 3
backlog[3].points = 8

sprint1 = AgileSprint("Sprint 1", capacity=8)
planned_stories = sprint1.plan_sprint(backlog)

print(f"Sprint: {sprint1.name}")
print(f"Velocity: {sprint1.velocity} points")
print("Stories in sprint:")
for story in planned_stories:
    print(f"  - {story.title} ({story.points} points)")
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates agile sprint planning where user stories are prioritized and selected into sprints based on team capacity. It shows the agile principle of adaptive planning - the team commits to stories based on their velocity and priority.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) for sorting backlog

examples.title: Agile Daily Standup Simulation
examples.description: Java code simulating an agile daily standup meeting structure
examples.explanation: This Java code demonstrates the agile daily standup meeting concept. Each team member quickly reports what they did yesterday, what they plan to do today, and any blockers they face. This enables quick coordination and early identification of impediments, supporting the agile principle of daily collaboration.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = number of team members
